skill.name: authorship-credit-gen
skill.description: Generates fair and transparent authorship assignments following ICMJE guidelines and CRediT taxonomy. Helps research teams determine author order, assign CRediT contributor roles, document individual contributions, and resolve authorship disputes in multi-institutional research.
skill.summary: Assign fair author order and CRediT contributor roles for research manuscripts using ICMJE guidelines
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 82 potential issues (score 100/100), triggering AI review. Manual evaluation confirmed ALL 82 findings are false positives. The 38 'weak cryptographic algorithm' detections are CRediT role dictionary keys (C1-C14) misidentified as crypto algorithms. The 39 'shell backtick execution' detections are Markdown code formatting backticks in documentation. The 3 'C2 keyword' detections match the CRediT role code 'C2' (Data curation), not command-and-control infrastructure. The 3 'hardcoded URL' detections are an XML namespace identifier string (never fetched) and a source repository link. The 2 file write detections are legitimate document generator output saves. No network calls, no data exfiltration, no unauthorized command execution, no prompt injection, and no malicious intent were found. Two minor concerns noted: unescaped XML output in generate_xml() and user-controlled file write paths without validation, both expected behavior for this type of tool.

low_findings.title: Unescaped XML Output in Author Names
low_findings.description: The generate_xml() method at scripts/main.py:223-252 embeds author names and affiliations directly into XML without escaping characters such as <, >, &. If author names contain these characters, the XML output will be malformed. Low risk because the output is consumed by the user, not submitted to an external system.

low_findings.title: User-Controlled File Write Paths
low_findings.description: File writes at scripts/main.py:350 and scripts/main.py:440 accept user-provided file paths without sanitization or path traversal checks. Expected behavior for a document generator where the user controls their output destination. Low risk because the user runs the script locally and chooses where to save output.

content.user_title: Generate Fair Research Authorship Credit Assignments
content.value_statement: Determining author order and contributor roles for research manuscripts is time-consuming and often leads to disputes. This skill generates transparent authorship assignments using ICMJE guidelines and CRediT taxonomy, helping teams document contributions fairly and resolve conflicts before journal submission.
actual_capabilities: Generate ranked author order lists based on weighted contribution criteria
Map individual contributions to the official CRediT 14-role taxonomy
Detect contribution inequities and flag potential authorship disputes before submission
Generate journal-ready contributor statements in text, JSON, XML, and bilingual formats
Validate role assignments against the standard CRediT vocabulary
Export formatted output compatible with Nature, Science, and other major journal requirements
limitations: Does not resolve actual interpersonal authorship disputes between researchers
Requires manual input of each author contributions and cannot auto-detect contributions from manuscripts
Only supports the CRediT 14-role taxonomy and ICMJE guidelines, not other authorship frameworks
Output quality depends on accurate and honest contribution reporting by the research team

use_cases.title: Assign Author Order for Multi-Institutional Paper
use_cases.description: Determine fair author ranking for a manuscript with 5 to 15 collaborators across multiple institutions using weighted contribution criteria and ICMJE guidelines.

use_cases.title: Prepare CRediT Statement for Journal Submission
use_cases.description: Generate a CRediT contributor statement in the format required by the target journal, mapping each author contributions to the standard 14-role taxonomy.

use_cases.title: Audit Contribution Equity Across Projects
use_cases.description: Review contribution distribution across research team members to identify inequities, flag authors below the substantial contribution threshold, and recommend resolution steps before manuscript submission.

prompt_templates.title: Simple Author Order for Small Team
prompt_templates.prompt: I have a manuscript with three authors: Dr. Chen contributed conceptualization, methodology, and writing the original draft. Dr. Roberts contributed data curation, formal analysis, and manuscript revision. Dr. Zhang contributed investigation, validation, and resources. Generate a fair author order using ICMJE guidelines.
prompt_templates.scenario: You have a small research team and need to determine author order quickly before submission

prompt_templates.title: CRediT Statement for Journal Format
prompt_templates.prompt: Map these contributions to CRediT roles: Author 1 led study design, collected data, and drafted the manuscript. Author 2 performed statistical analysis, prepared figures, and revised the manuscript. Author 3 recruited patients, validated results, and secured funding. Generate a journal-ready CRediT statement in text format.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need a properly formatted CRediT statement to include with your journal submission

prompt_templates.title: Equity Check for Large Research Team
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze the contribution distribution for our 7-author paper and check for equity issues. Flag any author with less than 15 percent contribution weight. For each flagged author, suggest whether their role should be expanded, their position adjusted, or whether they belong in the acknowledgments section instead.
prompt_templates.scenario: A team member raised concerns about contribution fairness and you want to audit before submission

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Format Export for International Collaboration
prompt_templates.prompt: Our manuscript has co-authors from China, Germany, and the United States. Generate the contributor statement in four formats: plain text for the manuscript body, JSON for our institutional repository, JATS XML for the journal submission system, and bilingual English-Chinese format for our international co-authors to review.
prompt_templates.scenario: You need contributor statements in multiple formats for different institutional and journal requirements

output_examples.input: Map contributions for three authors: Dr. Smith contributed study design, methodology, and manuscript drafting. Dr. Jones contributed data analysis, figure preparation, and manuscript revision. Dr. Lee contributed experiments, validation, and software development.
output_examples.output:
Author Contributions
====================

Dr. Smith: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing - original draft
Dr. Jones: Formal analysis, Visualization, Writing - review & editing
Dr. Lee: Investigation, Validation, Software

output_examples.input: Check contribution equity for a 4-author paper where one author only contributed resources and one review round
output_examples.output:
Equity Analysis Results:

- Dr. Smith: 4 roles - substantial contribution confirmed
- Dr. Jones: 3 roles - substantial contribution confirmed
- Dr. Lee: 3 roles - substantial contribution confirmed
- Dr. Park: 1 role - WARNING: below substantial contribution threshold

Recommendation: Dr. Park current contribution may not meet ICMJE authorship criteria. Consider expanding their role in the revision or moving them to the Acknowledgments section.

best_practices: Document all author contributions at the start of the research project and update the record as the work progresses
Discuss and agree on authorship order with every co-author before generating the final contributor statement
Use the equity analysis feature to identify potential disputes early, before the manuscript is submitted to a journal
anti_patterns: Do not assign authorship based only on seniority or institutional hierarchy without documenting each person actual research contributions
Do not generate the final authorship statement without review and written agreement from every listed author
Do not use the tool to justify excluding legitimate contributors who meet ICMJE criteria from the author list

faq.question: What is the CRediT taxonomy?
faq.answer: CRediT (Contributor Roles Taxonomy) is a 14-role standard that describes each contributor specific role in a research project. It is used by many journals to provide transparent and machine-readable author contribution statements.

faq.question: Can this skill handle non-English author names and affiliations?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill supports bilingual output in English and includes full support for international author names, affiliations, and character sets.

faq.question: How does the tool determine author order?
faq.answer: The tool calculates author order using weighted contribution criteria including intellectual input, execution effort, writing contribution, and supervision. You can adjust the weights to match your team priorities and field-specific norms.

faq.question: Does this skill guarantee compliance with my target journal requirements?
faq.answer: The skill follows ICMJE guidelines and CRediT taxonomy, which are widely accepted by major journals. Always verify the specific formatting requirements of your target journal before submission.

faq.question: What happens if two authors contributed equally to the work?
faq.answer: The skill supports equal contribution designations. You can mark multiple authors as equal contributors, and this designation will be clearly noted in the generated contributor statement.

faq.question: Can I export the results directly to my journal submission system?
faq.answer: The skill generates output in text, JSON, and JATS XML formats. Most journal submission systems accept one or more of these formats for contributor statements. You may need to copy the text format into the submission form.
